We stand at the threshold of God's final and most terrible judgment against Egypt. What Moses announces in this chapter is not merely another plague—it is the death of the firstborn across an entire nation, from the palace to the slave quarters, from human children to livestock. The specificity is devastating: every firstborn, without exception, will die at midnight. Pharaoh's own son, the heir to his throne and the continuity of his dynasty, will be taken. The cry that rises from Egypt will be unlike anything the world has ever heard. This is not punishment scaled to fit a crime; this is judgment meant to break a nation's will entirely. God makes clear that He does not merely punish sin—He demonstrates His absolute sovereignty over life and death itself. When the Lord says "I will bring one more plague," we must hear it as it is: an act of divine power so complete that it will finally compel Pharaoh to release His people, not out of negotiation, but out of total defeat.

Yet before this judgment falls, God commands something that seems almost contradictory: the Israelites are to ask their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold, and remarkably, they will receive it. The Lord has given the people favor in the sight of the very nation about to be devastated. Moses himself is honored by Pharaoh's officials and the people. This is the strange mercy woven through judgment—God does not simply destroy; He provides for His people even as His wrath falls on their oppressors. The Egyptians will give willingly, almost desperate to appease the God whose power they cannot resist. What God is establishing here is that liberation is not theft but a rightful claim, and the plundering of Egypt is part of Israel's redemption. Those who will mourn their dead will simultaneously empty their treasuries for the freedom of those they enslaved.

The heart of Pharaoh, however, remains hard. Verses 9 and 10 return us to a truth that has echoed through these plagues: the Lord Himself hardens Pharaoh's heart so that His wonders might be multiplied. This is not a new hardening but a continuation of what God has been doing from the beginning. Pharaoh has repeatedly refused to release Israel, and with each refusal, God sends another plague, each one greater than the last. The hardening is real; Pharaoh's resistance is real; yet both serve God's purpose of displaying His power throughout the earth. In this chapter, Moses speaks God's word "hot with anger," aware of what is coming, aware that Pharaoh will not listen, aware that judgment is now inevitable. The ten plagues have not been a negotiation leading to compromise—they have been a sustained demonstration of who God is and what He will do to free His people.

What should strike us most deeply is that God announces this final plague with complete certainty. There is no question of whether it will happen, no contingency in the Lord's promise. The distinction He will make between Egypt and Israel—where the cry of death rises in one nation while not even a dog barks in the Israelite camp—reveals that God's sovereignty extends to the precise details of judgment and deliverance. Pharaoh's officials will come and bow before Moses, begging for Israel to leave. This is not a negotiation; it is surrender. Before we ever reach the Passover itself, we must understand that the God of the Bible does not bargain with those who oppose His purpose. He judges. He delivers. And He does so with power that no human resistance can withstand.
